cards.grammarNote: Possession in Arabic is expressed by attaching a suffix directly to the noun. ـِي (-ī) = my. The noun loses its tanwin (nunation) when a suffix is added: بَيْت + ي = بَيْتِي.
ـكَ (-ka) = your (masculine). The case ending of the noun (here -u nominative) shows before the suffix: اِسْمُ + كَ = اِسْمُكَ.
ـكِ (-ki) = your (feminine). The only difference from the masculine suffix ـكَ is the kasra vowel, giving ـكِ. In unvowelled text they look identical — context clarifies.
ـهُ (-hu) = his. After a nominative noun the suffix is -hu: صَدِيقُهُ. After a genitive noun: صَدِيقِهِ (-hi). The vowel on the suffix harmonizes with the preceding vowel.
ـهَا (-hā) = her. Unlike the masculine suffix, the feminine 3rd person suffix does not change with case: always -hā.
ـنَا (-nā) = our. This suffix is the same for all genders and is invariable: بَيْتُنَا (our house), كِتَابُنَا (our book).
ـكُمْ (-kum) = your (masculine plural). For a feminine plural group: ـكُنَّ (-kunna). ـكُمْ is the default form used in MSA for mixed or unspecified groups.
ـهُمْ (-hum) = their (masculine/mixed group). For a feminine-only group: ـهُنَّ (-hunna). Compare: بَيْتُهُمْ (their house, mixed group) vs بَيْتُهُنَّ (their house, women only).
أَب (father) is one of five special nouns whose vowel changes with case: أَبُوكَ (nominative), أَبَاكَ (accusative), أَبِيكَ (genitive). These are called the Five Nouns (الأسماء الخمسة).
When adding a suffix to a feminine noun ending in ة (taa' marbuta), the ة becomes ت: لُغَة + نَا = لُغَتُنَا. The taa' marbuta always 'opens up' before a suffix.
